step_by_step_thinking:
The key point regarding working time lies in understanding when manipulation can occur safely while maintaining optimal results. For alginate, the working time starts once the gelation process begins — specifically, after the initial chemical reaction involving calcium sulfate and trisodium phosphate completes, leading to the formation of calcium sulfate and sodium sulfate. At this point, the material becomes sufficiently elastic to allow for handling. The text explicitly states that "the second stage begins after all the trisodium phosphate has been consumed," marking the beginning of gelation, and notes that this period provides "optimum working time" for manipulating alginate. Once the material achieves adequate elasticity—typically observed through tactile feedback—it is ready to be loaded into the tray and placed in the patient’s mouth. Prior to reaching this elasticity phase, the material remains too fluid and unstable for safe manipulation. The working time ends precisely when the material acquires enough elasticity to support proper placement and retention during impression taking. After achieving this property, further manipulation would compromise quality and integrity.
Therefore, the correct description of working time aligns with option A: “After the start of appearing elastic properties of impression material.”
